title:
4926. Design a Vending Machine Leasing Management System
System DesignHigh-Level Design (HLD)ScalabilityDatabase Design

transcript.text:
4926. Design a Vending Machine Leasing Management System
System DesignHigh-Level Design (HLD)ScalabilityDatabase Design
Hard
Design a high-level architecture for a company that leases vending machines to businesses across multiple cities. The system needs to manage machine inventory, track lease contracts and business entities, monitor real-time health and stock status of machines, and automate billing based on lease terms.

blocks.md:
I'll start streaming the speakable opener right now — no tools, no math yet — so you can begin talking. Capacity math comes right after.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, then draw the high-level design one requirement at a time — inventory, leases, telemetry, billing — and finally go deep on the two or three hardest parts, the real-time telemetry firehose and correct automated billing. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

**The core tension:** this is really *two systems in one box*. There's a small, money-critical, strongly-consistent world — businesses, lease contracts, invoices — that wants Postgres and ACID. And there's a huge, lossy, high-volume world — hundreds of thousands of machines screaming telemetry every minute — that wants a stream and a time-series store. The whole design is about keeping those two worlds separate but stitched together so billing stays correct while telemetry stays cheap.

> 🏢 I'll target a **Uber-style real-time / ops bar** — real-time device state, a clear machine **state machine**, per-city sharding, and field-ops failure modes — since that's the interesting part. If you'd rather I tilt toward pure enterprise CRUD or cost, tell me and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **FR1 — Manage machine inventory & lifecycle.** Operators should be able to register a machine and track its status/location through a lifecycle: `in_warehouse → deployed → leased → maintenance → retired`.
- **FR2 — Manage business entities & lease contracts.** Operators should be able to onboard a business (lessee) and create/update/terminate a lease that binds machines to a business with terms (rate, billing cycle, start/end).
- **FR3 — Ingest & monitor real-time machine health + stock.** Machines should stream telemetry (online/offline heartbeat, temperature, stock levels, fault codes); the system surfaces live status and **raises alerts** on low-stock / fault / offline.
- **FR4 — Automate billing from lease terms.** The system should generate invoices on each lease's cycle (flat lease fee + optional usage/commission), and charge them.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** field-tech dispatch/routing, the consumer vend/payment transaction itself (we only meter it), tax engine internals, the mobile app UI, contract e-signature, procurement.

**Non-Functional (talkable targets, NOT yet computed):**
- **Availability:** control-plane (leases, billing) **99.9%** ("three nines"); telemetry ingest **99.95%** but **lossy-tolerant** — dropping a heartbeat is fine, dropping an invoice is not.
- **Latency:** dashboard reads **p99 < 300ms**; alert fire (fault → operator notified) **< 30s end to end**; billing is a **batch** job, not latency-sensitive (minutes is fine).
- **Consistency:** **strong** for leases/invoices/payments (financial correctness, read-your-own-writes); **eventual** for telemetry/live-status dashboards (a few seconds stale is fine).
- **Scale:** hundreds of thousands of machines, hundreds of cities, tens of thousands of businesses; telemetry is the firehose, contracts/invoices are small.
- **Durability:** **zero loss** for financial data (RPO ≈ 0); telemetry RPO of minutes is acceptable.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** billing correctness under late-arriving or duplicated telemetry (usage-based charges) is the subtle risk — I'll address it with idempotent invoice generation and a billing cut-off watermark in the deep dive.

> 💬 *"Before I draw anything — the thing I want to get right is that this is two systems glued together: a tiny ACID world for money, and a massive eventual-consistency world for device telemetry. Almost every decision falls out of that split."*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So at its heart we've got four flows: manage the physical machine inventory and its lifecycle, manage businesses and their lease contracts, ingest real-time health and stock telemetry with alerting, and automate billing off the lease terms. The non-functionals split cleanly — leases and invoices need strong consistency and basically can't lose data, because that's money. Telemetry is the opposite: huge volume, but I can afford to drop the occasional heartbeat. That asymmetry is what drives the whole architecture."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Questions I'd ask (and why each changes the design):**
- **How many machines, and how often do they report?** → This sets ingest QPS and whether I need a stream + time-series DB or can get away with plain Postgres. *(Biggest design lever.)*
- **Is billing purely flat lease fee, or usage-based (per-vend / revenue share)?** → Usage-based means telemetry feeds billing, which raises the correctness bar enormously (late/duplicate events corrupt invoices).
- **Do machines have reliable connectivity, or do they go offline and batch-upload?** → Drives buffering, out-of-order handling, and "is offline a fault or normal?"
- **Who are the actors/tenants?** → Internal operators, lessee businesses (self-serve portal?), field techs. Affects authz model and multi-tenancy.
- **Regulatory / data residency per city or country?** → Could force per-region sharding of contract & PII data.
- **Do we take the payment, or hand invoices to an external processor?** → Build vs. buy on the payment rail.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:**
- **500K machines**, heartbeat + stock snapshot **every 60s** (worst case I'll consider 10s).
- **~50K businesses**, **~100K active leases**, **~100K invoices/month**.
- Billing = **flat monthly lease fee + optional usage commission** (so telemetry *can* touch billing → I must handle it carefully).
- Machines have intermittent connectivity; offline ≤ N minutes is normal, beyond that is an alert.
- We **don't** process the consumer's card; we **meter** vends and either invoice the business or take commission. Invoices are charged via an **external payment processor** (Stripe-style).
- Multi-city, single primary region to start, designed to shard by city/region.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — 500K machines on a 60-second heartbeat, flat fee plus a usage commission so telemetry feeds billing. Before I size it, is usage-based billing actually in scope, or should I treat billing as purely flat-fee? That one toggle changes how careful I have to be on the telemetry path."

Now let me size the few numbers that actually change a decision.

blocks.name: run_command

input.command:
cat > capacity.py << 'EOF'
machines = 500_000

# --- Telemetry ingest ---
for interval_s, label in [(60, "60s heartbeat"), (10, "10s worst-case")]:
    qps = machines / interval_s
    print(f"Telemetry ingest @ {label:16s}: {qps:,.0f} events/s")

interval_s = 60
qps = machines / interval_s
event_bytes = 200
# storage
events_per_day = machines * (86400/interval_s)
raw_per_day_gb = events_per_day * event_bytes / 1e9
raw_per_year_tb = raw_per_day_gb * 365 / 1000
print(f"\nTelemetry events/day: {events_per_day/1e6:,.0f} M/day")
print(f"Telemetry raw storage: {raw_per_day_gb:,.0f} GB/day  -> {raw_per_year_tb:,.1f} TB/year (raw, 1x)")
# kafka throughput
mbps = qps * event_bytes / 1e6
print(f"Stream throughput: {mbps:,.1f} MB/s ingest")

# --- Relational (money) world ---
leases = 100_000
businesses = 50_000
lease_row = 500   # bytes incl terms
invoice_row = 400
invoices_year = 100_000 * 12
rel_lease_gb = (leases*lease_row + businesses*1000 + machines*300) / 1e9
rel_invoice_gb = invoices_year * invoice_row * 5 / 1e9  # 5 yrs retention
print(f"\nRelational lease/biz/machine metadata: {rel_lease_gb*1000:,.0f} MB total")
print(f"Invoices (5yr retention): {rel_invoice_gb*1000:,.0f} MB total")
print("-> Money-world data fits in a single Postgres node trivially.")

# --- Read side ---
operators = 2000
reads_per_op_s = 0.5
dash_qps = operators*reads_per_op_s
print(f"\nDashboard read QPS (steady): ~{dash_qps:,.0f}/s  (tiny)")

# --- Billing batch ---
print(f"\nBilling: {invoices_year/12:,.0f} invoices/month as a batch job (not latency-critical)")
EOF
python3 capacity.py

blocks.output:
Telemetry ingest @ 60s heartbeat   : 8,333 events/s
Telemetry ingest @ 10s worst-case  : 50,000 events/s

Telemetry events/day: 720 M/day
Telemetry raw storage: 144 GB/day  -> 52.6 TB/year (raw, 1x)
Stream throughput: 1.7 MB/s ingest

Relational lease/biz/machine metadata: 250 MB total
Invoices (5yr retention): 2,400 MB total
-> Money-world data fits in a single Postgres node trivially.

Dashboard read QPS (steady): ~1,000/s  (tiny)

Billing: 100,000 invoices/month as a batch job (not latency-critical)

blocks.md:
## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'm deferring full estimation — I'll only compute what changes a decision, and do the rest inline later. Here are the few that matter:

| Metric | Value | Rounded / say-out-loud | Decision it forces |
|---|---|---|---|
| Telemetry ingest (60s) | 8,333 events/s | **~8K/s** | Needs a stream + TSDB, not raw Postgres |
| Telemetry ingest (10s worst case) | 50,000 events/s | **~50K/s** | Confirms: must shard ingest |
| Telemetry raw storage | 52.6 TB/yr | **~50 TB/yr** | Dominant cost → downsample + tiered retention |
| Stream throughput | 1.7 MB/s | **<2 MB/s** | A single Kafka topic handles this easily |
| Lease/business/machine metadata | 250 MB | **trivial** | One Postgres node |
| Invoices (5-yr) | 2.4 GB | **trivial** | One Postgres node |
| Dashboard reads | ~1,000/s | **~1K/s** | A read replica + cache is plenty |

**The ONE number that forces the architecture:** **~8K telemetry events/s steady, ~50K at a 10-second cadence** — that's 3–4 orders of magnitude more than the *money* side (a few hundred invoices/day, ~1K dashboard reads/s). **Flip threshold:** below ~5K events/s I'd skip the stream and write telemetry straight to a time-series table in Postgres; above it, I commit to **Kafka + a time-series DB**. We're above it.

> 🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — the request rate)*, **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on a cached/stored value)*, **TSDB** *(time-series database — a store optimized for timestamped metrics, cheap to append and to range-query over time)*.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is telemetry: about eight thousand events a second on a one-minute heartbeat, and up to fifty thousand if devices report every ten seconds. Compare that to the money side — a few hundred invoices a day and maybe a thousand dashboard reads a second — and you see the asymmetry. So telemetry gets its own pipeline, a stream into a time-series store, and the storage there, around fifty terabytes a year raw, is my dominant cost, which I'll tame with downsampling. The contracts-and-invoices data is a rounding error — a couple of gigabytes — it lives happily on one Postgres node."

---

## 4. Core Entities

First draft of the key nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later:

- **Business (Lessee)** — the company we lease to; owns leases and gets invoiced.
- **Machine** — a physical vending unit; has a lifecycle state and a current location.
- **Lease (Contract)** — binds one or more machines to a business with terms (rate, cycle, dates).
- **TelemetryEvent** — one timestamped reading from a machine (health, stock, faults).
- **MachineState** — the *current* live snapshot of a machine (latest stock/health/online) — distinct from the raw event stream.
- **Alert** — a raised condition (low stock, fault, offline) needing operator/field action.
- **Invoice** — a generated bill for a lease's billing period; has line items and a status.
- **UsageRecord** — metered vends/revenue for a lease period (the bridge from telemetry to billing).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The nouns are Business, Machine, Lease, and then on the telemetry side a raw TelemetryEvent stream plus a current MachineState snapshot, an Alert, and on the money side an Invoice and a UsageRecord that bridges metered usage into billing. This is just the first pass — I'll nail down the actual fields and types when I get to the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS; the caller is always identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token proving who's calling — we read the operator/business id from it, never trust a client-supplied id)*. Going requirement by requirement:

**FR1 — Inventory**
```
POST /machines                  {serial, model, location}        -> 201 {machineId}    # register
PATCH /machines/{id}            {state, location}                -> 200                # lifecycle transition
GET  /machines/{id}             -> {machine + current state}                           # read
GET  /machines?city=&state=     -> paged list                                          # operator console
```
Why: POST creates a unit; PATCH does the guarded state-machine transition; GETs feed the console.

**FR2 — Businesses & Leases**
```
POST /businesses                {name, billingInfo}              -> 201 {businessId}
POST /leases                    {businessId, machineIds[], rate, cycle, startDate}  -> 201 {leaseId}
   Header: Idempotency-Key      # so a retried "create lease" doesn't double-bind machines
PATCH /leases/{id}              {terms... , status: terminated}  -> 200
GET  /leases/{id}               -> {lease + machines + status}
```
Why: leases are money-binding, so the create carries an **Idempotency-Key**; PATCH handles renewal/termination.

**FR3 — Telemetry & Monitoring** (device-facing, high volume)
```
POST /telemetry                 {machineId, ts, health, stock, faultCodes}  -> 202 Accepted
   (device auth via mTLS cert; ts is device clock but we stamp server-receive too)
GET  /machines/{id}/status      -> latest MachineState                       # dashboard
GET  /alerts?city=&severity=    -> open alerts
WS   /alerts/stream             -> push live alerts to operator console
```
Why: telemetry returns **202** (accepted, not yet durably processed — it's async); status/alerts are reads; WebSocket pushes live alerts so we don't poll.

**FR4 — Billing**
```
POST /billing/run              {periodId}        -> {jobId}        # admin/cron-triggered batch
GET  /invoices/{id}            -> {invoice + line items + status}
POST /invoices/{id}/pay        Idempotency-Key   -> {paymentStatus}  # charge via processor
```
Why: billing is a batch trigger; the pay endpoint is idempotent so a retry never double-charges.

> 💬 *"Two things I'm deliberate about: telemetry returns 202 because it's fire-and-forget into a queue, and every money-mutating endpoint — create lease, pay invoice — carries an Idempotency-Key so retries can't double-bind or double-charge. I never trust a client-supplied business id or price; I read identity from the JWT and pull terms from the lease record server-side."*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The API splits the same way the system does. The device side is one fat endpoint, POST /telemetry, returning 202 because we just accept it onto a queue. The money side — leases and invoice payments — uses idempotency keys so retries are safe, and I always derive the price from the stored lease, never from the request body. Everything else is straightforward REST reads for the operator console, plus a WebSocket to push alerts live."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Palette: client = apps/devices, svc = our services, async = queues/streams, store = datastores, ext = third-party.

### 6.1 "Manage machine inventory & lifecycle"

The simplest slice: a service that registers machines and guards their lifecycle transitions in an ACID store.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory Service | Stateless service | Owns machine CRUD + state-machine validation | — |
| Machine DB | PostgreSQL | ACID; transitions must be atomic & valid (no `retired → leased`) | Single-writer scaling, fine at this size |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  OP[Operator console]:::client -->|1 POST/PATCH machine| INV[Inventory Svc]:::svc
  INV -->|2 validate transition + write| MDB[(Machine DB Postgres)]:::store
  INV -->|3 emit machine-event| BUS[[Event bus]]:::async
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: none yet (this is the foundation).

1. Operator POSTs a new machine or PATCHes its state.
2. Inventory Service validates the transition against the allowed state machine and writes atomically to Postgres.
3. It emits a `machine-event` (e.g. `deployed`) on the bus so other services (billing, monitoring) react.

> 💬 *"I'll model the machine as an explicit state machine and enforce it in a transaction — you can't lease a retired machine — so the database is the source of truth for physical state."*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Start dead simple: an Inventory Service in front of Postgres. The key idea is the machine is a state machine — in_warehouse, deployed, leased, maintenance, retired — and I validate every transition inside a transaction so we never get an illegal state. When state changes, I emit an event so billing and monitoring stay in sync without tight coupling."

### 6.2 "Manage business entities & lease contracts"

Now bind machines to businesses with contractual terms — also ACID, and it must atomically reserve machines.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lease Service | Stateless service | Owns business + lease CRUD, enforces terms | — |
| Lease DB | PostgreSQL (same cluster) | ACID across lease ↔ machine binding; financial source of truth | — |
| Idempotency store | Redis (TTL 24h) | Dedup retried lease-create so machines bind once | Lost on crash (re-creatable) |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  OP[Operator console]:::client -->|1 POST lease + Idem-Key| LS[Lease Svc]:::svc
  LS -->|2 check key| RID[(Idempotency Redis)]:::store
  LS -->|3 tx bind machines + write lease| LDB[(Lease DB Postgres)]:::store
  LS -->|4 reserve machines| MDB[(Machine DB Postgres)]:::store
  LS -->|5 emit lease-activated| BUS[[Event bus]]:::async
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Machine DB**, **Event bus** (from §6.1).

1. Operator POSTs a lease with an Idempotency-Key.
2. Lease Service checks the key in Redis; a duplicate returns the original result.
3–4. In one transaction it writes the lease and flips the bound machines to `leased` (cross-table, same Postgres cluster → real ACID).
5. Emits `lease-activated`, which billing subscribes to so the next cycle bills this lease.

> 💬 *"The lease create and the machine reservation must be one atomic unit — otherwise I could bill a business for a machine that's still in the warehouse. Same Postgres cluster lets me do that in a single transaction."*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Leases are the money-binding step, so two things matter. One, the create is idempotent via a Redis key, so a retried request never double-binds machines. Two, writing the lease and reserving its machines happen in a single Postgres transaction — they're either both done or neither — so we never bill for an unreserved machine. On success I emit lease-activated, and billing picks it up from there."

### 6.3 "Ingest & monitor real-time machine health + stock"

The firehose. ~8K–50K events/s must land durably-enough, update a live snapshot, feed dashboards, and trigger alerts — without ever blocking on the slow stores.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest Gateway | Stateless, mTLS | Authenticates devices, validates, drops to queue fast (202) | — |
| Telemetry Stream | **Kafka** (partitioned by machineId) | Durable buffer; absorbs spikes; decouples ingest from consumers; preserves per-machine order | Operational overhead |
| Stream Processor | **Flink** (or Kafka consumers) | Stateful: dedup, update snapshot, evaluate alert rules | Stateful ops complexity |
| TSDB | **Timescale / Cassandra** | Cheap append + time-range queries for history/graphs | Not for ad-hoc joins |
| State Store | **Redis** (current snapshot per machine) | O(1) "latest status" for dashboards | Volatile (rebuildable from stream) |
| Alert Service | Stateless + WS | Fans alerts to operators; dedups/debounces | — |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  DEV[Machines IoT]:::client -->|1 POST telemetry mTLS| GW[Ingest Gateway]:::svc
  GW -->|2 produce| K[[Kafka by machineId]]:::async
  K -->|3 consume| FP[Stream Processor Flink]:::svc
  FP -->|4 append raw| TS[(TSDB Timescale)]:::store
  FP -->|5 upsert latest| RS[(State Redis)]:::store
  FP -->|6 rule breach -> alert| AL[Alert Svc]:::svc
  AL -->|7 push| OP[Operator console]:::client
  RS -->|8 read status| OP
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Operator console** (from §6.1).

1. Machine POSTs telemetry over mTLS; gateway validates and returns **202** immediately.
2. Gateway produces to Kafka, **partitioned by machineId** so one machine's events stay ordered.
3. Stream Processor consumes.
4. Appends the raw event to the TSDB (history/graphs).
5. Upserts the machine's **current** snapshot in Redis (live dashboard reads).
6. Evaluates alert rules (low stock, fault code, no-heartbeat-in-N-min) and, on breach, calls Alert Service.
7. Alert Service pushes to the operator console over WebSocket (and SMS/email).
8. Dashboard reads "latest status" straight from Redis — never touches the firehose path.

> 💬 *"Key move: ingest returns 202 and just produces to Kafka. Everything slow — writing history, updating snapshots, evaluating alerts — happens downstream, so a slow database can never back-pressure the devices."*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "This is the firehose, so the design rule is: accept fast, process async. The gateway authenticates the device, drops the event onto Kafka partitioned by machine id, and returns 202 — done. Downstream, a stream processor fans it three ways: raw history into a time-series DB, the latest snapshot into Redis for instant dashboard reads, and alert-rule evaluation that pushes faults and low-stock warnings to operators within seconds. Redis as the 'current state' cache means the dashboard never has to scan the event history."

### 6.4 "Automate billing from lease terms"

A batch that, each cycle, turns leases (+ metered usage) into idempotent invoices and charges them — correctness over speed.

| Component | Technology | Why | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Billing Scheduler | Cron / Airflow | Triggers per-cycle runs; retriable | — |
| Billing Service | Stateless workers | Computes line items, generates invoices idempotently | — |
| Usage Aggregator | Batch over TSDB | Rolls metered vends into a `UsageRecord` per lease/period | Depends on telemetry completeness |
| Invoice DB | PostgreSQL (money cluster) | ACID; invoice is financial truth | — |
| Payment Processor | **Stripe** (external) | Don't build a card rail; PCI offload | Vendor dependency |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  CR[Scheduler cron]:::svc -->|1 trigger run| BS[Billing Svc]:::svc
  LDB[(Lease DB Postgres)]:::store -->|2 read active leases| BS
  TS[(TSDB Timescale)]:::store -->|3 aggregate usage| BS
  BS -->|4 idempotent insert invoice| IDB[(Invoice DB Postgres)]:::store
  BS -->|5 charge Idem-Key| PAY[Stripe]:::ext
  PAY -->|6 webhook paid/failed| BS
  BS -->|7 emit invoice-issued| BUS[[Event bus]]:::async
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
  classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe;
```
↳ reuses existing: **Lease DB** (§6.2), **TSDB** (§6.3), **Event bus** (§6.1).

1. Scheduler triggers the billing run for a period.
2. Billing reads active leases.
3. For usage-based terms, the Usage Aggregator rolls metered vends from the TSDB into a UsageRecord (with a **cut-off watermark** — only events before the period close count).
4. Inserts the invoice with a **unique (leaseId, periodId)** key → re-running the job never creates a duplicate invoice.
5. Charges via Stripe with an idempotency key.
6. Stripe webhook reports paid/failed; Billing updates status and schedules dunning on failure.
7. Emits `invoice-issued` for notifications/reporting.

> 💬 *"Two safety rails: invoices are keyed on lease-plus-period so the batch is rerunnable, and usage uses a cut-off watermark so a late telemetry event doesn't silently change last month's bill."*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Billing is a batch job where correctness beats speed. A scheduler kicks off a run; we read active leases, aggregate any usage from the time-series DB up to a fixed cut-off, and write each invoice with a unique lease-plus-period key so the job is safely re-runnable — re-running never double-bills. We charge through Stripe with an idempotency key, listen for the paid-or-failed webhook, and kick off dunning if a charge fails. We never build our own card rail."

---

**Final (high-level)** — the reconciled union of all four slices:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Clients
    OP[Operator console]:::client
    DEV[Machines IoT]:::client
  end
  subgraph Control plane money
    INV[Inventory Svc]:::svc
    LS[Lease Svc]:::svc
    BS[Billing Svc]:::svc
    MDB[(Machine DB)]:::store
    LDB[(Lease DB)]:::store
    IDB[(Invoice DB)]:::store
    RID[(Idempotency Redis)]:::store
  end
  subgraph Telemetry plane firehose
    GW[Ingest Gateway]:::svc
    K[[Kafka by machineId]]:::async
    FP[Stream Processor Flink]:::svc
    TS[(TSDB)]:::store
    RS[(State Redis)]:::store
    AL[Alert Svc]:::svc
  end
  BUS[[Event bus]]:::async
  CR[Scheduler]:::svc
  PAY[Stripe]:::ext

  OP --> INV --> MDB
  OP --> LS --> LDB
  LS --> RID
  LS --> MDB
  DEV --> GW --> K --> FP
  FP --> TS
  FP --> RS
  FP --> AL --> OP
  RS --> OP
  CR --> BS
  LDB --> BS
  TS --> BS
  BS --> IDB
  BS --> PAY --> BS
  INV --> BUS
  LS --> BUS
  BS --> BUS
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
  classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe;
```
Load-bearing columns: **Machine DB**(`state`, `city` shard key), **Lease DB**(`status`, `terms`, `(businessId)`), **Invoice DB**(`UNIQUE(leaseId, periodId)`, `status`), **TSDB**(`(machineId, ts)`), **State Redis**(`machine:{id} -> snapshot, TTL`).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Zoom out and you literally see two planes. The control plane — inventory, leases, billing on Postgres — is small, transactional, and strongly consistent: that's the money. The telemetry plane — gateway, Kafka, stream processor, time-series DB, and a Redis snapshot — is big, async, and eventually consistent: that's the devices. The only bridge between them is the billing job reading aggregated usage out of the time-series DB, and an event bus that keeps lifecycle changes in sync. Keeping those planes separate is what lets each scale on its own terms."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The two richest deep dives are the telemetry firehose — ordering, dedup, hot shards — and billing correctness under late or duplicate events. Which do you want first, or is there another corner you'd rather probe?"

---

## 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields (type — note) | Store | Shard/Partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Business | id (uuid), name, billingInfo (jsonb), status | PostgreSQL | by region | Strong |
| Machine | id, serial, model, **state** (enum), city, lat/lng, currentLeaseId | PostgreSQL | by city | Strong |
| Lease | id, businessId, **terms** (jsonb: rate, cycle, commission%), startDate, endDate, status | PostgreSQL | by businessId/region | Strong |
| LeaseMachine | leaseId, machineId (M:N bind) | PostgreSQL | — | Strong |
| TelemetryEvent | machineId, **ts**, serverTs, health(jsonb), stock(jsonb), faultCodes[] | **TSDB (Timescale)** | (machineId, ts) | Eventual |
| MachineState | machineId → latest snapshot, lastSeen | **Redis** (TTL ~10m) | machineId | Eventual (rebuildable) |
| Alert | id, machineId, type, severity, status, openedAt | PostgreSQL | by city | Strong |
| UsageRecord | leaseId, periodId, vends, revenue (computed) | PostgreSQL | (leaseId, periodId) | Strong |
| Invoice | id, leaseId, periodId, lineItems(jsonb), amount, **status**, **UNIQUE(leaseId, periodId)** | PostgreSQL | by businessId | Strong |

**Why each store:**
- **PostgreSQL** for business/machine/lease/invoice — *the access pattern is relational with multi-row transactions (bind machines + write lease, charge + update invoice) and it's the financial source of truth; ACID + read-your-own-writes are non-negotiable, and the data is tiny (~GBs).*
- **TSDB (TimescaleDB)** for telemetry — *append-heavy, time-range queried ("last 24h stock for machine X", "aggregate vends for period"); native downsampling/retention beats Postgres for 50 TB/yr.*
- **Redis** for MachineState — *pure key→latest-snapshot lookups at dashboard speed; volatile is fine because it's rebuildable by replaying recent Kafka.*
- **Kafka** as the durable buffer (not a database) — *decouples ingest from processing and gives replay.*

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage follows access patterns. The lease, machine, and invoice tables go in Postgres because they need multi-row ACID transactions and they're the financial truth — and they're small, just gigabytes. Raw telemetry goes in a time-series DB because it's append-only and I query it by time range, plus it downsamples old data automatically, which matters at fifty terabytes a year. The current machine status sits in Redis as a simple latest-snapshot lookup — and it's safe to lose because I can rebuild it by replaying recent Kafka. Each store earns its place by the way I read and write it."

---

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two-planes split and the bottleneck — "money plane needs ACID, telemetry plane needs throughput" — and reason forward from whichever the question touches.

### How do we ingest 8K–50K telemetry events/s without losing data or blocking devices?

#### Bad: Devices write straight to the database (synchronous)
**Approach:** Each machine POSTs and the service writes directly to Postgres/TSDB before responding.
**Challenges:** At 8K–50K writes/s the DB is the bottleneck; a slow query back-pressures devices, they retry, and you get a **retry storm** that takes the whole fleet's ingest down. ⚠️ **Trap:** treating telemetry like normal CRUD — synchronous DB writes on the device hot path is the classic blow-up.

#### Good: Buffer through Kafka, async consumers
**Approach:** Gateway returns 202 and produces to Kafka; consumers write TSDB/Redis at their own pace. Kafka absorbs spikes and gives durability + replay.
**Challenges:** Need partitioning for parallelism and **ordering**; a single hot machine (or a hot *city* partition) can lag one consumer.

#### Great: Kafka partitioned by machineId + idempotent stream processor
**Approach:** Partition by `machineId` (per-machine order preserved, fleet load spread across partitions). Stream Processor is **idempotent** — dedups on `(machineId, ts)` so a device's at-least-once retries don't double-count. Scale consumers horizontally with the partition count; cold-start/replay just re-reads the log. Rebuild Redis snapshots from the stream after any cache loss.
**Trade-offs:** Kafka ops overhead and you must pick partition count ahead of peak. This is what real IoT platforms do.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  DEV[Machines IoT]:::client -->|produce at-least-once| K[[Kafka by machineId]]:::async
  K -->|partition 0..N| FP[Stream Processor Flink]:::svc
  FP -->|dedup machineId,ts| TS[(TSDB)]:::store
  FP -->|upsert latest| RS[(State Redis)]:::store
  classDef client fill:#1b2436,stroke:#6b7a90,color:#dde6f2;
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef async fill:#2a1e08,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#fde68a;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
```

**Bottleneck math:** one Kafka partition handles well over our 1.7 MB/s *total*, but a single consumer thread tops out ~a few K events/s — so at 50K/s I need **~16–32 partitions** to parallelize. Hot **city** shards (a dense metro) skew load, so I partition by `machineId` not city to spread it.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I never let a device wait on a database. Telemetry goes onto Kafka, partitioned by machine id so each machine's events stay in order while the fleet's load spreads across partitions for parallelism. The consumer is idempotent — it dedups on machine-id-plus-timestamp — so the device's at-least-once retries can't double-count. If Redis dies, I rebuild the live snapshots by replaying the log. That's the standard IoT ingestion shape."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why partition by machineId, not city?":** "City partitioning concentrates a dense metro's traffic onto one partition and one consumer — a hot shard. machineId spreads load evenly and still preserves the only ordering I care about, which is per-machine."

### How do we keep automated billing correct under late or duplicate telemetry?

#### Bad: Bill live off whatever usage exists at run time
**Approach:** At billing time, sum vends from the TSDB as-is.
**Challenges:** Telemetry is at-least-once and can arrive late (a machine was offline and batch-uploads). Duplicates inflate the bill; late events mean last month's invoice was wrong. ⚠️ **Trap:** charging real money off an eventually-consistent stream with no cut-off.

#### Great: Cut-off watermark + idempotent invoices + dedup usage
**Approach:**
- **Watermark/cut-off:** a period closes at time T; only events with `serverTs ≤ T` (plus a grace window) count. Anything later rolls into the *next* period as an adjustment — the closed invoice is immutable.
- **Dedup:** UsageRecord aggregates over deduplicated `(machineId, ts)` events.
- **Idempotent invoice:** `UNIQUE(leaseId, periodId)` — re-running the batch is a no-op for already-issued invoices.
- **Idempotent charge:** Stripe idempotency key per (invoiceId) so a retried charge never double-bills.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  TS[(TSDB dedup events)]:::store -->|<= watermark T| AGG[Usage Aggregator]:::svc
  AGG -->|UsageRecord| BS[Billing Svc]:::svc
  BS -->|UNIQUE leaseId,periodId| IDB[(Invoice DB)]:::store
  BS -->|Idem-Key per invoice| PAY[Stripe]:::ext
  classDef svc fill:#10261a,stroke:#34d399,color:#d1fae5;
  classDef store fill:#0c1322,stroke:#52617a,color:#cbd5e1;
  classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#ede9fe;
```
**Trade-off:** late events become next-period adjustments rather than retroactive edits — slightly less "accurate to the second" but invoices are immutable and auditable, which is what finance needs.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Billing money off an eventually-consistent stream is dangerous, so I add three guards. A cut-off watermark freezes the period — late events become next month's adjustment instead of rewriting a closed invoice. The usage aggregation runs over deduplicated events. And the invoice itself is keyed on lease-plus-period, with an idempotent Stripe charge, so the whole batch is safely re-runnable and a retry never double-charges. Immutable, auditable invoices are what finance actually wants."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what about a machine offline for a week then dumping data?":** "Those events land after the watermark, so they don't corrupt the closed invoice — they're picked up as a reconciling line item in the next cycle, and I can flag large back-fills for manual review."

### How do we detect a machine going offline (absence of events)?

#### Great: Heartbeat + timeout sweep (or Flink timers)
**Approach:** Each event refreshes `lastSeen` in Redis with a TTL, or a Flink timer per machine. A periodic sweep (or the timer firing) flags machines with no heartbeat in N minutes as `offline` and raises an alert. Absence-of-signal can't be detected by the event path alone — you need a timer.
**Trade-off:** tune N vs. false alarms for flaky connectivity; debounce so a 1-packet blip doesn't page an operator.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Offline is the absence of data, which an event-driven pipeline can't see directly — so I use timers. Every heartbeat refreshes a last-seen timestamp; a per-machine timer or a sweep flags anyone silent for more than N minutes. I debounce it so a single dropped packet doesn't page someone, and I tune N against how flaky real connectivity is."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just poll every machine?":** "Polling 500K machines is its own firehose and wastes device battery/data; passive heartbeat-timeout scales for free off the stream I already have."

### How do we scale the operator dashboard reads?

Jump straight to **Great:** reads hit **Redis** (latest MachineState) for live status and a **read replica** of Postgres for lease/invoice lists, with results cached. At ~1K reads/s this is trivially served; the firehose never touches this path. Hot list queries ("all low-stock machines in city X") are answered from a maintained Redis set updated by the stream processor.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Dashboard reads are tiny — about a thousand a second — and they never touch the firehose. Live status comes from the Redis snapshot, lease and invoice lists from a Postgres read replica, and hot filters like 'low-stock machines in this city' are pre-maintained as Redis sets by the stream processor, so the operator console stays sub-300-millisecond without any heavy queries."

---

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**Availability:** control plane **99.9%** (multi-AZ Postgres with sync standby); telemetry ingest **99.95%** (stateless gateways behind an LB, Kafka replication factor 3).

**Per-dependency graceful degradation:**
- **Kafka down:** gateway buffers to local disk / returns 503 to devices, which retry with backoff — telemetry is lossy-tolerant, no money impact.
- **TSDB down:** Kafka retains the backlog (days of retention); consumers catch up later — dashboards go stale, billing waits.
- **Redis (state) down:** dashboards lose "live" view; rebuild snapshots by replaying Kafka. No financial impact.
- **Stripe down:** invoices still generate (immutable); charges queue and retry via dunning.
- **Postgres primary down:** failover to sync standby (RTO ~1 min); no committed financial data lost.

**RPO/RTO per data class:**
- Financial (lease/invoice): **RPO ≈ 0** (synchronous replication), **RTO ~1 min**, with **tested PITR restores**.
- Telemetry: **RPO minutes** (Kafka-backed), RTO best-effort.

**Multi-AZ vs multi-region:** multi-AZ everywhere for HA; go multi-region only if data residency or DR mandates it — then single-write-region for the money plane (avoid multi-master on financial data) with async replica for read/DR.

**Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **telemetry storage + stream**, not the money DB. ~50 TB/yr raw → with downsampling (keep raw 30d, rolled-up 2y) the hot footprint is far smaller; ballpark **low-five-figures/month** for Kafka + TSDB + storage, vs. **hundreds/month** for the Postgres money cluster. **Optimization:** aggressive downsampling and tiered storage (hot → cold object storage) on telemetry is where the savings are.

**Observability:** SLO **burn-rate alerts** on ingest lag (Kafka consumer lag), alert-fire latency, and billing-run success; distributed tracing across gateway→stream→stores; runbooks for "consumer lag climbing" and "billing run failed."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The failure philosophy mirrors the two planes. On telemetry I optimize for graceful degradation — if Kafka or the time-series DB hiccups, devices retry and consumers catch up, and the worst case is a stale dashboard, never lost money. On the money plane I optimize for durability — synchronous Postgres replication, near-zero RPO, tested point-in-time restores, and immutable invoices. My dominant cost is telemetry storage, so that's where downsampling and tiered storage earn their keep."

---

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

- **Two-planes split (Postgres + Kafka/TSDB) vs. one general-purpose DB.** I gave up simplicity and added a stream to operate. *Reverses if* the fleet is small (<~5K events/s) — then a single Postgres with a time-series extension does everything and I'd drop Kafka. Tied to the 99.95% ingest SLO.
- **Cut-off watermark (late events → next period) vs. retroactive correction.** I gave up to-the-second usage accuracy for immutable, auditable invoices. *Reverses if* the business demands exact retroactive billing — then invoices become versioned with credit/debit adjustments, more complex but precise.
- **Partition by machineId vs. by city.** I gave up city-local locality for even load. *Reverses if* queries are overwhelmingly per-city and connectivity is regional — then city-partitioning plus per-city consumers might win, at the risk of hot metros.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: splitting into two planes buys scale at the cost of operating a stream — and I'd collapse it back to one Postgres if the fleet were small. The billing watermark trades second-by-second accuracy for immutable invoices, which I think finance prefers, but it's reversible to versioned adjustments. And partitioning telemetry by machine spreads load evenly at the cost of city locality. None are permanent — each flips at a clear scale or requirement change."

---

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. Why separate the telemetry store from the contract store at all?**
Because their access patterns and consistency needs are opposite: contracts are small, relational, multi-row-transactional, and money-critical (ACID); telemetry is 8K–50K writes/s, append-only, time-ranged, and loss-tolerant. Forcing both into one store either bankrupts you on a Postgres telemetry firehose or sacrifices ACID on money. Splitting lets each scale and fail independently.
> 💬 *"Same box, opposite needs — money wants ACID, telemetry wants throughput, so they get different stores."*

**2. How do you guarantee an invoice is never generated twice?**
A `UNIQUE(leaseId, periodId)` constraint makes invoice insertion idempotent, so re-running the batch is a no-op for already-issued periods, and the Stripe charge carries a per-invoice idempotency key so a retried charge can't double-bill. The billing run is therefore safely retriable end to end. Failures resume from where they stopped without side effects.
> 💬 *"Lease-plus-period is unique, and the charge is idempotent — so the whole batch is rerunnable."*

**3. A machine is offline for a week, then uploads a week of vends — what happens to billing?**
Those events arrive after the period watermark, so they don't mutate the already-closed, immutable invoice; they're aggregated into the next period as a reconciling line item. Large back-fills get flagged for review to catch tampering or meter faults. This keeps invoices auditable while still capturing the revenue.
> 💬 *"Closed invoices are immutable — late data becomes next month's adjustment, not a retroactive edit."*

**4. How do you detect 'offline' when offline means no messages arrive?**
Absence-of-signal needs a timer, not an event: each heartbeat refreshes a `lastSeen`, and a per-machine timer (Flink) or a sweep flags machines silent beyond N minutes. We debounce to avoid paging on a single dropped packet, and tune N to real connectivity. Polling all machines would be its own firehose, so passive timeout wins.
> 💬 *"Offline is the absence of data, so I detect it with a heartbeat-timeout timer, not the event path."*

**5. What stops a hot city from overwhelming one consumer?**
We partition Kafka by `machineId`, not city, so a dense metro's load spreads evenly across partitions instead of piling onto one. Per-machine ordering — the only ordering we need — is still preserved. If a single machine ever became hot (misconfigured firmware spamming), rate-limiting at the gateway caps it.
> 💬 *"machineId partitioning spreads the hot metro across the cluster while keeping per-machine order."*

**6. How do you handle out-of-order telemetry?**
Events carry both device `ts` and server `serverTs`; the TSDB is keyed on `(machineId, ts)` so out-of-order inserts still land in the right time bucket, and the stream processor keeps the *latest* snapshot by max-ts so a delayed old event can't overwrite newer state. Billing uses serverTs against the watermark for fairness. Dedup on `(machineId, ts)` absorbs retries.
> 💬 *"I key history by machine-and-timestamp and guard the live snapshot with max-timestamp, so stale events can't clobber fresh state."*

**7. Why Kafka and not a simpler queue like SQS?**
We need durable retention and **replay** (to rebuild Redis snapshots and reprocess for new alert rules) plus per-key ordering and high fan-out to multiple independent consumers — Kafka gives all of that as a log. SQS is great for at-least-once work queues but doesn't retain a replayable, ordered log. At our throughput Kafka's partition model is also the cleanest scaling story.
> 💬 *"I picked Kafka for replay and per-machine ordering — SQS can't replay a log or preserve order."*

**8. How do you enforce machine lifecycle correctness (no illegal transitions)?**
The machine is modeled as an explicit state machine and every transition is validated inside a Postgres transaction, so you can't, say, lease a retired unit or bind a machine to two active leases. The lease-create binds machines in the same transaction as the lease write. Illegal transitions are rejected at the service boundary with a clear error.
> 💬 *"Machine state is a guarded state machine enforced transactionally — illegal jumps just can't commit."*

**9. How would you add usage-based / revenue-share billing later?**
The bridge already exists: the Usage Aggregator rolls metered vends from the TSDB into a UsageRecord per lease-period, and the lease `terms` jsonb can carry a commission percentage. Adding it is mostly a new line-item calculator in the Billing Service plus the watermark/dedup guarantees we already built. The clean lease→usage→invoice seam is what makes the extension cheap.
> 💬 *"Usage billing slots into the existing UsageRecord-to-invoice seam — it's a new line-item rule, not new plumbing."*

**10. How do you secure device ingest against spoofing?**
Each machine authenticates with a per-device **mTLS** client cert (mutual TLS — both sides prove identity), so a random POST can't impersonate a machine; the gateway also rate-limits per device and validates payload schema. Certs are rotatable and revocable if a unit is compromised or decommissioned. Telemetry is signed at the device identity level, not trusted by claimed machineId.
> 💬 *"Devices authenticate with mutual-TLS certs, so you can't spoof a machine id by just POSTing JSON."*

**11. What's your consistency story for read-your-own-writes on the operator console?**
For money operations (create lease, issue invoice) we read from the Postgres primary right after write, so an operator always sees their own change immediately. For live telemetry the console reads the eventually-consistent Redis snapshot, which may lag a few seconds — acceptable and labeled "as of last seen." We never promise strong consistency on device state.
> 💬 *"Money reads hit the primary for read-your-own-writes; device status is explicitly 'last seen,' eventual by design."*

**12. How does billing scale as machines grow 10x?**
The billing batch is embarrassingly parallel — partition leases by businessId/region and run workers concurrently, each writing idempotent invoices, so 10x machines just means more workers, not a redesign. The usage aggregation reads the TSDB which already scales horizontally. The Postgres invoice table stays small (~tens of GB) even at 10x.
> 💬 *"Billing parallelizes by business and the invoice data stays tiny, so 10x is just more workers."*

**13. What happens to active leases when a machine breaks and is swapped?**
The lease binds to a logical slot; we transition the broken machine to `maintenance`, unbind it, and bind a replacement in one transaction so the lease's machine count and billing stay consistent. The swap emits events so monitoring tracks the new unit. Billing continues uninterrupted because it bills the lease, not the specific serial.
> 💬 *"Leases bill the slot, not the serial — so a swap is an atomic unbind-rebind and billing never blinks."*

**14. How do you prevent alert storms (a city loses power, 5K machines go offline)?**
The Alert Service deduplicates and **correlates** — 5K offline machines in one city/segment collapse into one "regional outage" alert rather than 5K pages, using debounce windows and grouping keys. Operators get one actionable signal with a drill-down. This keeps on-call sane during correlated failures.
> 💬 *"I correlate and group alerts, so a citywide power cut is one outage alert, not five thousand pages."*

---

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is two systems in one. A small, strongly-consistent money plane — businesses, leases, invoices on Postgres with ACID transactions, idempotency keys, and immutable invoices — and a big, eventually-consistent telemetry plane — half a million machines streaming roughly eight thousand events a second through a gateway that returns 202, onto Kafka partitioned by machine id, into a stream processor that writes raw history to a time-series DB, the latest snapshot to Redis, and fires alerts to operators within seconds. The only bridges between the planes are an event bus for lifecycle changes and a billing batch that aggregates metered usage from the time-series DB behind a cut-off watermark, then issues idempotent invoices and charges them through Stripe. The whole design falls out of one asymmetry: I can drop a heartbeat, but I can never drop a dollar — so telemetry is built for throughput and graceful degradation, and money is built for ACID and zero data loss."
